content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

November 8, 2017

James D. Ciampa
Lagerlof Senecal Gosney & Kruse, LLP
301 North Lake Avenue, 10th Floor
Pasadena, CA 91101-4108

Re:    Your Request for Advice
       Our File No. A-17-115

Dear Mr. Ciampa:

        This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of La Habra City Attorney Richard Jones regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act")¹ and Government Code Section 1090. Please note that we do not advise on any other area of law, including Public Contract Code or common law conflicts of interest. We are also not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate.

        Regarding our advice on Section 1090, we are required to forward your request and all pertinent facts relating to the request to the Attorney General's Office and the Orange County District Attorney's Office, which we have done. (Section 1097.1(c)(3).) We did not receive a written response from either entity. (Section 1097.1(c)(4).) We are also required to advise you that, for purposes of Section 1090, the following advice "is not admissible in a criminal proceeding against any individual other than the requestor." (See Section 1097.1(c)(5).)

QUESTION

        May La Habra City Attorney Richard Jones handle the annexation negotiations between the City and Orange County, given that he also serves as chairman of the California Domestic Water Company, which previously provided water services to some of the areas being annexed?

CONCLUSION

        No. As discussed below, Mr. Jones has a conflict of interest under the Act in the annexation decision.

_________________________

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-17-115
Page No. 2

FACTS

Richard Jones is the La Habra City Attorney. In addition, he is chairman of the California
Domestic Water Company's ("Cal Domestic,") Board of Directors, and receives compensation in
that role. As a general practice, Mr. Jones recuses himself from any discussions of water-related
issues that arise at the city of La Habra (the "City"), that may involve Cal Domestic.

Cal Domestic provides wholesale water service to its shareholders. Among Cal Domestic's
shareholders is the City, which purchases water from Cal Domestic and then re-sells that water to
its customers.

The City is in the beginning stages of annexing certain areas that are now a part of
unincorporated Orange County. The City desires that Mr. Jones handle the negotiations of the
annexation agreement between Orange County and the City.

Some of the properties being annexed previously received water service from Cal Domestic
and are now receiving water service from the City under a water service maintenance agreement
between the City and Cal Domestic. This agreement was negotiated and otherwise dealt with by
outside counsel (outside counsel was brought in to handle the transfer of those services when
Mr. Jones recused himself from the transfers and related negotiations). Once the annexation is
completed, water service to those properties will be permanently transferred to the City under the
prior agreement. Any issues concerning the transfer of those water services will be handled by that
outside counsel.

In a telephone conversation on November 6, 2017, you noted that Cal Domestic entered into
the transfer agreement with the City in anticipation of the annexation, that Cal Domestic is
changing its organization to become a water wholesaler, and has decided to cease retail water
supply service. However, if the annexation did not proceed for some reason, Cal Domestic could
resume water service to those retail customers.

ANALYSIS

Conflict of Interest under the Act:

Section 87100 prohibits any state or local public official from making, participating in
making, or using his or her official position to influence a government decision in which the official
has a financial interest. As pertinent to the facts provided, interests under the Act that are potentially
disqualifying include:

• Any business entity in which the public official has a direct or indirect investment worth at
least $2,000 and any business entity in which the public official is a director, officer,
partner, trustee, employee, or holds any position of management. (Section 87103(a) and (d).)

• Any source of income, such as a business entity, from which the official has received
income of $500 or more within 12 months before the decision. This also includes income
from any client of the business entity of at least $500, provided to and received by the public
official within 12 months before the decision is made. (Section 87103(c).)

File No. A-17-115
Page No. 3

Mr. Jones has an interest in Cal Domestic as a business entity pursuant to Section 87103(d) based on his position as Chairman of Cal Domestic's Board of Directors. Mr. Jones also has an interest in Cal Domestic as a business entity.

Foreseeability

A financial effect is presumed to be reasonably foreseeable when it is explicitly involved in a decision. Financial interests that are explicitly involved include an interest that is a named party in, or subject of, a government decision. (Regulation 18701(a).) Based on the facts provided, the Company is not explicitly involved in the annexation agreement, although we note that the issues of water service and the annexation are inter-related. For interests that are not explicitly involved, as here, the effect need not be likely to be considered reasonably foreseeable. "In general, if the financial effect can be recognized as a realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or theoretical, it is reasonably foreseeable." (Regulation 18701(b).)

Materiality

1. Financial Interests in Business Entities: The Act and its interpretive regulations provide different materiality standards that apply to determine whether a reasonably foreseeable financial effect on an interest will be material. (Regulation 18702.) Regulation 18702.1 applies to interests in business entities.² Generally, a reasonably foreseeable financial effect on a business entity in which the official has an interest (and that is not explicitly involved in a decision), is material:

"[I]f a prudent person with sufficient information would find it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision's financial effect would contribute to a change in the price of the business entity's publicly traded stock, or the value of a privately held business entity." (Regulations 18702.1(b) and 18702.3(a)(4).)

2. Special Nexus Rule: In addition to the general standards, Regulation 18702.3(c) provides:

"Any reasonably foreseeable financial effect on a person³ who is a source of income to a public official is deemed material if the public official receives or is promised the income to achieve a goal or purpose which would be achieved, defeated, aided, or hindered by the decision."

The rationale for the nexus test is that when an employee earns a salary to accomplish a purpose that may be advanced by what he or she does as a public official, we presume that the private employer is benefiting from the actions of the employee in his or her official capacity. (Yarnell Advice Letter, No. A-00-161.) Typically, a "nexus" is found in situations where the official is also a high-level employee with direct influence and control over his or her employer's management or policy decisions.

_______________

² Regulation 18702.3 sets forth the standard for materiality applicable to the effect of a decision on an official's interest in a source of income, however, it provides that if the source of income is a business entity, materiality is also determined pursuant to the standards of Regulation 18702.1.

³ "Person" includes a corporation. (Section 82047.)

File No. A-17-115
Page No. 4

The annexation, among other things, will ultimately result in water service to those affected properties, once provided by Cal Domestic, being permanently transferred to the City under a water service maintenance agreement with Cal Domestic. Alternatively, the water service may revert to Cal Domestic if the annexation is not completed. Considering Cal Domestic's stake in the annexation, a prudent person would find it reasonably foreseeable that the decisions would change the value of Cal Domestic. Moreover, Mr. Jones holds a high-level position at Cal Domestic and the goals or purposes of Cal Domestic may be achieved, defeated, aided, or hindered by the annexation decision such as its goal of curtailing retail water service. The foreseeable effect on Cal Domestic is also material under the nexus rule. Therefore, Mr. Jones has a conflict of interest in the annexation decision and may not take part in the decision.

Government Code Section 1090:

Under Section 1090, "the prohibited act is the making of a contract in which the official has a financial interest." (People v. Honig (1996) 48 Cal.App.4th 289, 333.) A contract that violates Section 1090 is void. (Thomson v. Call (1985) 38 Cal.3d 633, 646.) Section 1090 is concerned with financial interests, other than remote or noninterests, that prevent public officials from exercising absolute loyalty and undivided allegiance in furthering the interests of their agencies. (Sigall v. Taft (1962) 58 Cal.2d 565, 569.) The analysis of a financial interest under Section 1090 is separate and distinct from that applied under the Act.

In light of our conclusion that Mr. Jones has conflict of interest under the Act and must recuse himself from participating in the decision under the Act, further advice under Section 1090 is not necessary. For purposes of Section 1090, when an employee, rather than a board member, is financially interested in a contract, the employee's agency is prohibited from making the contract by Section 1090 only if the employee was involved in the contract-making process. As long as the employee plays no role whatsoever in the contracting process (either because participation is outside the scope of the employee's duties or because the employee disqualifies himself or herself from participation), the employee's agency is not prohibited from contracting with the employee or the business entity in which the employee is interested. (See 80 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 41 (1997).)

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Brian G. Lau
Assistant General Counsel

By: Zachary W. Norton
Senior Counsel, Legal Division

ZWN:jgl

Conflict of Interest under the Act:

making, or using his or her official position to influence a government decision in which the official

least $2,000 and any business entity in which the public official is a director, officer, 
partner, trustee, employee, or holds any position of management. (Section 87103(a) and (d).) 
• Any source of income, such as a business entity, from which the official has received 
income of$500 or more within 12 months before the decision. This also includes income 
from any client of the business entity of at least $500, provided to and received by the public 
official within 12 months before the decision is made. (Section 87103(c).)  
Mr. Jones has an interest in Cal Domestic as a business entity pursuant to Section 87103(d) 
based on his position as Chairman of Cal Domestic's Board of Directors. Mr. Jones also has an 
interest in Cal Domestic as a business entity.

a decision. Financial interests that are explicitly involved include an interest that is a named party 
in, or subject of, a government decision. (Regulation 1870l(a).) Based on the facts provided, the

different materiality standards that apply to determine whether a reasonably foreseeable financial

business entities.2 Generally, a reasonably foreseeable financial effect on a business entity in which

reasonably foreseeable that the decision's financial effect would contribute to a 
change in the price of the businesses entity's publicly traded stock, or the value of 
a privately held business entity." (Regulations 18702.1 (b) and l 8702.3(a)(4).) 
2. Special Nexus Rule: In addition to the general standards, Regulation 18702.3(c) provides: 
"Any reasonably foreseeable financial effect on a person3 who is a source 
of income to a public official is deemed material if the public official receives or

(Yarnell Advice Letter, No. A-00-161.) Typically, a "nexus" is found in situations where the 
official is also a high-level employee with direct influence and control over his or her employer's 
management or policy decisions. 
2 Regulation 18702.3 sets forth the standard for materiality applicable to the effect of a decision on an official's 
interest in a source of income, however, it provides that if the source of income is a business entity, materiality is also 
detennined pursuant to the standards of Regulation 18702.1. 
3 "Person" includes a corporation. (Section 82047.)  
The annexation, among other things, will ultimately result in water service to those affected 
properties, once provided by Cal Domestic, being permanently transferred to the City under a water 
service maintenance agreement with Cal Domestic. Alternatively, the water service may revert to 
Cal Domestic if the annexation is not completed. Considering Cal Domestic's stake in the 
annexation, a prudent person would find it reasonably foreseeable that the decisions would change 
the value of Cal Domestic. Moreover, Mr. Jones holds a high-level position at Cal Domestic and the 
goals or purposes of Cal Domestic may be achieved, defeated, aided, or hindered by the annexation

Section 1090 is void. (Thomson v. Call (1985) 38 Cal.3d 633, 646.) Section 1090 is concerned with 
financial interests, other than remote or noninterests, that prevent public officials from exercising 
absolute loyalty and undivided allegiance in furthering the best interests of their agencies. (Stigall v. 
Taft (1962) 58 Cal.2d 565, 569.) The analysis of a financial interest under Section 1090 is separate

In light of our conclusion that Mr. Jones has conflict of interest under the Act and must
